hook: You already know how to solve systems of linear equations, and you've seen that some systems have unique solutions while others have infinitely many or none at all. But here's a deeper question: given a set of vectors, is every vector in the set truly contributing something new, or is one of them secretly just a combination of the others? For example, if $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\end{bmatrix}$, and $\mathbf{v}_3 = \begin{bmatrix}2\\3\end{bmatrix}$, is $\mathbf{v}_3$ really giving us any new direction, or is it just $2\mathbf{v}_1 + 3\mathbf{v}_2$? How can we make this idea of 'redundancy' mathematically precise?
concept: A set of vectors is **linearly independent** if no vector in the set can be written as a linear combination of the others — each one points in a genuinely new direction. Formally, vectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k$ are linearly independent if the only solution to $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{0}$ is $c_1 = c_2 = \cdots = c_k = 0$; otherwise they are linearly dependent.
anatomy: The key equation to test linear independence is the **vector equation** $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{0}$. The **scalars** $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k$ are what we solve for; they are called the **weights** or **coefficients**. The **trivial solution** is $c_1 = c_2 = \cdots = c_k = 0$ — this always satisfies the equation and tells us nothing interesting. A **nontrivial solution** is one where at least one $c_i \neq 0$, which is exactly the witness that the set is linearly dependent. We test for independence by forming the **coefficient matrix** $A = [\mathbf{v}_1 \ \mathbf{v}_2 \ \cdots \ \mathbf{v}_k]$ and row-reducing the homogeneous system $A\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$.

worked_example.description_latex: We want to determine whether these three vectors are linearly independent. Our strategy is to set up the equation $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + c_3\mathbf{v}_3 = \mathbf{0}$ and check whether the only solution is the trivial one.

We form the coefficient matrix $A$ by placing each vector as a column. Solving $A\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$ is exactly equivalent to finding all linear combinations of the columns that produce the zero vector, which is precisely what we need to check.

We row-reduce the matrix using elimination. Subtracting $2R_1$ from $R_2$ and $3R_1$ from $R_3$ clears the first column below the pivot, and then subtracting $R_2$ from $R_3$ clears the second column. Each row operation preserves the solution set of the homogeneous system.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{The row-echelon form has a pivot in every column: columns 1, 2, and 3.}
worked_example.description_latex: Because every column contains a pivot, there are no free variables in the system $A\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$. When there are no free variables, the only solution is the trivial solution $c_1 = c_2 = c_3 = 0$, which is the hallmark of linear independence.

Since the only solution to $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + c_3\mathbf{v}_3 = \mathbf{0}$ is $c_1 = c_2 = c_3 = 0$, none of the three vectors can be expressed as a combination of the other two — each one contributes a genuinely new direction.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to test whether $\{\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2\}$ is linearly independent. We set up the equation $c_1\mathbf{u}_1 + c_2\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{0}$ and form the coefficient matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 3\\2 & 6\end{bmatrix}$, which we will row-reduce.

After subtracting $2R_1$ from $R_2$, we get a zero row. Notice that column 2 has no pivot, meaning $c_2$ is a free variable. Now it is your turn: write out the general solution for $c_1$ and $c_2$ from this row-reduced system, and use it to determine whether the vectors are linearly independent or dependent.

Because $c_2$ is free, we can choose $c_2 = 1$ and get $c_1 = -3$, giving a nontrivial solution $-3\mathbf{u}_1 + 1\cdot\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{0}$. This means $\mathbf{u}_2 = 3\mathbf{u}_1$, confirming the set is linearly dependent — one vector is a scalar multiple of the other, so no new direction is introduced.

practice_problems.answer_latex: Yes, linearly independent. The only solution to $c_1\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}+c_2\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\end{bmatrix}=\mathbf{0}$ is $c_1=c_2=0$.
Linearly dependent, since $\mathbf{v}_1 = 2\mathbf{v}_2$, giving the nontrivial solution $c_1=1,\ c_2=-2$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Test whether $\mathbf{v}_1=\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_2=\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_3=\begin{bmatrix}2\\3\\1\end{bmatrix}$ are linearly independent by row-reducing $[\mathbf{v}_1\ \mathbf{v}_2\ \mathbf{v}_3]$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Linearly dependent. Row reduction gives a zero row (no pivot in column 3), so there is a free variable and a nontrivial solution exists. Specifically, $\mathbf{v}_3 = 2\mathbf{v}_1 + 3\mathbf{v}_2$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Suppose $A$ is a $3\times 4$ matrix. Without doing any computation, explain why the columns of $A$ must be linearly dependent, and state which theorem justifies this.
practice_problems.answer_latex: The columns of $A$ are four vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$. By the theorem that any set of more than $n$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is linearly dependent, the four columns must be linearly dependent (there will always be at least one free variable when $k > n$).

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A data scientist has three feature vectors $\mathbf{f}_1=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{f}_2=\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\3\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{f}_3=\begin{bmatrix}4\\5\\5\end{bmatrix}$ representing measurements. Are these features linearly independent, or is one redundant (a linear combination of the others)?
practice_problems.answer_latex: Linearly dependent: $\mathbf{f}_3 = 2\mathbf{f}_1 + \mathbf{f}_2$, so the third feature is redundant and contributes no new information independent of the first two.

common_mistakes: Students sometimes check only whether two of the vectors are scalar multiples of each other and conclude independence if they are not, missing the fact that a vector could still be a non-scalar linear combination of the others — for example, $\mathbf{v}_3 = 2\mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2$ makes the set dependent even though no two vectors are proportional. Always set up and solve the full homogeneous system.

After row reduction, students often declare a set linearly dependent only if the entire matrix reduces to the zero matrix, when in fact a single zero row (indicating one free variable) is already enough to guarantee dependence. The correct rule is: if any column lacks a pivot, the set is linearly dependent.

Students confuse linear independence with the vectors being orthogonal or 'different-looking.' Two vectors like $\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and $\begin{bmatrix}2\\2\end{bmatrix}$ clearly look different in size but are linearly dependent because one is a scalar multiple of the other. Independence is determined solely by whether the homogeneous equation has only the trivial solution, not by visual appearance.
untested_variants: Determining linear independence of a set that contains the zero vector (the set is always dependent, since $1\cdot\mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0}$ is a nontrivial solution).
Testing linear independence of polynomials or functions (e.g., $\{1, x, x^2\}$ in the vector space of polynomials) rather than column vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
Using the Wronskian to test linear independence of differentiable functions, a context encountered in differential equations.
Determining whether the rows of a matrix are linearly independent, and connecting this to the row rank of the matrix.
